dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at --Address Redacted--.
Hi ma'am, my name is Lynn, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Oh hello. I've been coughing and short of breath for about two weeks now. It's gotten a lot worse today.
Ma'am, can you tell me what date is today?
And how about your name?
What county are we in right now?
Can you show me by pointing at the sky, please?
Ma'am, we're going to quickly check your airway. Can you lean forward a bit?
Head and neck are symmetrical and without signs of trauma.
We're noticing some wheezing. It looks like you're having difficulty breathing. Do you currently use oxygen at home?
Skin is warm and dry.
Respirations are 24.
Oxygen saturation is at 87% with home oxygen on nasal cannula.
Pulse is strong and regular. I can feel the pulses in your hands and feet.
Blood pressure is 170 over 78.
Heart rate is 86.
Blood glucose is 108.
You mentioned coughing and shortness of breath. Are there any other symptoms?
Can you tell me what medications you're currently taking?
No, I don't have any known allergies.
What is your medical history?
Ma'am, can you say your name, tell me today's date, where you are, and what day of the week it is? That shows you're alert to person, place, time, and events.
Can you tell me when you last had something to eat or drink?
What seems to have brought on the recent worsening of your breathing?
Ma'am, would it be okay if we did a quick chest exam? I'm just going to listen to your lungs.
EKG machine is hooked up. We see sinus rhythm with some premature atrial and ventricular contractions and sinus arrhythmia.
Glasgow Coma Scale score is 15, with all components normal-eye opening 4, verbal response 5, and motor response 6.
We'll be assessing your end-tidal CO2 levels now for monitoring.
Initiating 4-lead EKG monitoring.
Lung sounds have improved slightly with wheezing still present in all lobes.
We are establishing IV access in your right hand for future medication administration.
Respiratory rate is now 26, with a heart rate of 64.
Blood glucose remains stable with no acute changes noted.
Administering 10mg dexamethasone IV. This will help reduce inflammation in your airways.
Magnesium sulfate 2g IV via infusion pump. This may help relax your airways and breathing.
Oxygen saturation is at 99%, blood pressure 138/85.

You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and events with no pain mentioned. You have a history of primary hypertension, hyperlipidema, diabetes insulin dependent, COPD, and mild asthma. Your chief complaint is breathing difficulties with associated cough and congestion for two weeks with worsening today.
Are you aware of any current illnesses or infections, such as possible bronchitis or the common cold?
You are on medication for bronchodilation, such as fluticasone in a daily reliever inhaler. Would you mind admitting to following that regimen?
Re-administering albuterol and Atrovent via Duo Neb at 6L with nasal cannula. We've previously given this and saw improvement.
Oxygen saturation is at 97% now, which is better than before with continued supportive care.
Do you have a specific hospital you'd prefer for transport?
We've secured you to the stretcher with five safety straps and the stretcher is securely loaded into the unit.
The patient will be transported once [COMPANY_NAME] can accept us. In the meantime, we will continue treatment in the hall while waiting.
Respiratory rate is around 24, still slightly elevated.
Heart rate is now 69.
Repeating albuterol and Atrovent into mask at 6L.
Re-evaluating breath sounds. Symmetrical chest movement, with wheezing still noted bilaterally.
Now your breathing is more controlled-respiratory rate at 17.
Blood pressure is now 151 over 80.
EKG continues to show multifocal PVCs and some signs of bigeminy and trigeminy. Let the hospital team know for further interpretation.
We're still waiting on site due to delays at [COMPANY_NAME] and no emergency rooms available yet.
Repeating the albuterol and Atrovent into mask at 6L as previously successful.
Sats are at 99% while on nasal cannula at 6L.
Blood pressure is 143 over 80.
Saturation drops to 98% during ongoing monitoring.
Arrived to find the patient in a tripod position with retractions and using 2-word sentences.
Oxygen saturation is now at 100% with continued nasal cannula support at 6LPM.
Blood pressure is 131 over 72.
Verified, you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
Atorvastatin is among the medications you're currently taking.
Respiratory rate is 21 and showing improved control.
You are also on medications including furosemide for heart failure, glipizide for diabetes control, and losartan for blood pressure. I see that you also take metoprolol for heart conditions.
Pain assessment verified at 0 - patient reports no pain.
Giving another dose of albuterol and Atrovent now.
Acute exacerbation likely due to possible bronchitis, a common cold, or a flare-up of pneumonia.
Securing patient for transport. [COMPANY_NAME] will be our final destination as requested by the patient.
You are on home oxygen via nasal cannula at 3L per minute normally. We have upped it to 6L for now with improvement in oxygen saturation.
The stretcher is ready for departure, and we are transporting you now.
